items.q: What happens in the recapitulation of sonata form?
items.correct: Both subjects return, but the second subject is now in the tonic key.
items.why: Students miss the KEY RESOLUTION. The exposition put subject 2 in a contrasting key; the recapitulation brings it back to the tonic, resolving the tonal tension - this is the dramatic point of sonata form.

items.correct: A recurring main theme (A) alternates with contrasting episodes (B, C, etc.).
items.misconception: A round where voices enter at different times.
items.why: Students confuse rondo (alternating refrain) with round (canon). Rondo: ABACA, ABACABA, etc. The A theme keeps coming back, like a refrain.

items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Students use 'classical' loosely. The Classical period specifically reacted against Baroque complexity - simpler textures, clearer cadences, and balanced phrase structures (Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven).

items.q: In the recapitulation, the second subject appears in:
items.why: It's Tonic. “Dominant” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's 1804. “1794” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Sonata form has how many main sections (excluding coda)?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
